behaviors.description: Renamed files appear in their own category and are not also counted as a deletion plus an addition.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is real git + CLI process behavior. Unit tests of rename-parsing logic can pass while real `git` emits rename status forms (for example similarity-prefixed rename records and path formatting) that the subprocess/stdout pipeline mis-parses. Integration with mocked git output can miss real rename detection behavior from the actual repo/index state. Integration-BRAKE: (a) yes, real subprocess/filesystem/git metadata needed; (b) no AI/ML; (c) no multi-agent chain; (d) no LLM stage orchestration. Rejection gate for higher levels: behavior text is purely git categorization; no model call or agent pipeline is described.
behaviors.test_description: Create a real temp git repo, commit baseline, perform a rename via git, run the CLI as a subprocess, parse stdout report, assert renamed count/category is correct and added/deleted counts do not include that same file.

behaviors.description: Diffs that include binary files do not cause the tool to crash; binary files are counted in the output.
behaviors.justification: Real failure appears at subprocess/encoding boundary with actual git binary-diff output and repository objects. Unit tests with synthetic strings miss byte/encoding/path edge cases and process-level failures. Integration with mocked diff provider can hide crashes that only occur when reading real command output from git. Integration-BRAKE: (a) yes, real subprocess/git output required; (b) no AI/ML; (c) no multi-agent chain; (d) no LLM handoff. Rejection gate for higher levels: behavior is deterministic CLI robustness for binary diffs, not model-dependent.
behaviors.test_description: In a real temp git repo, add/modify at least one binary file, run the CLI subprocess against the repo, assert exit code is success, no crash on stderr, and binary file is represented in report counts.

behaviors.description: Running the tool on a repository that has no commits yet produces an empty report rather than an error.
behaviors.justification: This depends on real unborn-branch behavior from git in an initialized repo with zero commits. Unit tests over internal functions cannot reproduce actual git exit statuses/messages; integration with mocked git client may falsely pass. Concrete miss above Integration: mocked repo layer may return empty diff, while real git command can return fatal revision errors unless handled. Integration-BRAKE: (a) yes, real git subprocess semantics; (b) no AI/ML; (c) no multi-agent chain; (d) no LLM orchestration. Rejection gate for higher levels: behavior text references only repo state and CLI output, not AI steps.
behaviors.test_description: Initialize a real temp git repo with no commits, run CLI subprocess, assert successful exit and structured empty report on stdout (no exception/error output treated as failure).

behaviors.description: The counts in the report match the actual change history of the repository being analyzed.
behaviors.justification: This is end-to-end correctness against real git history traversal. Unit tests can validate counting math but miss failures in history query wiring, revision-range handling, and subprocess output parsing. Integration with fake history adapters can hide discrepancies that only appear with real commit graph/state. Integration-BRAKE: (a) yes, real repository/process behavior needed; (b) no AI/ML; (c) no multi-agent chain; (d) no LLM orchestration. Rejection gate for higher levels: no behavior text indicates model judgment or agent pipeline.
behaviors.test_description: Construct a real repo history with known added/deleted/renamed/modified events across multiple commits, run CLI subprocess, and assert report counts exactly match independently computed expected counts from git history ground truth.

behaviors.description: The output is identical whether the changes being analyzed are staged or unstaged.
behaviors.justification: Correctness depends on real git index vs working-tree semantics. Unit tests of comparison logic do not exercise actual staged/unstaged plumbing; integration with mocked status data can hide differences caused by real git commands/options and index state. Concrete miss above Integration: fake staging state can be internally consistent while real git reports different file sets or statuses between index and worktree if command usage is wrong. Integration-BRAKE: (a) yes, real git/index/filesystem behavior required; (b) no AI/ML; (c) no multi-agent chain; (d) no LLM orchestration. Rejection gate for higher levels: behavior is deterministic CLI/git parity, not AI-dependent.
behaviors.test_description: In a real temp repo, create equivalent change scenarios once staged and once unstaged, run CLI subprocess for both states, and assert stdout structured report is byte-for-byte equivalent (or semantically identical after stable serialization).
